facts_disclosed: Startup pivoted to lending, payments deprecated

turns.content: ever have one of those months where the ground just shifts under you and you're supposed to act like it's all part of the plan
That sounds like something significant happened. What's going on?
so the company pivoted. like full pivot. we're doing lending now. payments is basically getting sunset
Wow, that's a huge shift. Payments to lending is a completely different domain. How did this come about?

investors pushed for it. the payments space is insanely crowded now, margins are razor thin. leadership looked at the numbers, agreed, and here we are. building a lending platform from basically scratch. i built that transaction pipeline from the ground up and now it's deprecated. rip, 2022 to 2025

That's a real loss, even if the business logic makes sense. Building something from the ground up, watching it scale, and then having it deprecated - that's a quiet kind of grief. You put real craft into that system.

How are you feeling about the lending side? Is it technically interesting or just another rewrite?

honestly the lending domain is more interesting technically. credit scoring, risk models, regulatory compliance with bafin. it's a harder problem. my brain likes harder problems. the payments stuff was elegant but kind of solved at this point. i'm not angry, more like — resigned but curious?

"Resigned but curious" is a pretty healthy place to land. Credit scoring and risk modeling are genuinely complex - way more algorithmic depth than moving money from point A to point B. And BaFin compliance for lending adds a whole regulatory dimension.

Are you on the core team building the new platform?

yeah, core team. which means every data model has to account for audit trails now. we're going event sourcing because regulators want to know exactly what happened at timestamp X and why. my schema diagrams look like conspiracy theory boards. genau

Ha! Schema diagrams that look like conspiracy boards - that's the BaFin effect. Event sourcing is the right call for regulated financial systems though. The immutable log approach is more work upfront but saves you from the nightmare of reconstructing state after the fact.

Sounds like you're approaching this pragmatically - mourning the old system a bit but seeing the technical opportunity in the new one.

pragmatic is my default setting. no point being sentimental about code nobody's going to maintain. the new stuff will be better anyway because i actually know what mistakes to avoid now. — kernel just knocked a pen off my desk btw, she's been extra chaotic today. probably senses the existential engineering grief

Kernel's providing real-time chaos engineering for your emotional state. Cats are great at grounding you by creating small, immediate problems.

But seriously, carrying lessons from the old system into the new one is the real value. The transaction pipeline isn't gone - it lives on as architecture instincts. The lending platform will be better because you built the payments system first.

architecture instincts. i like that. also the upside of event sourcing is that the lending platform is basically a git repo for money. every state change has a commit hash metaphorically speaking. if bafin asks what happened on march 3rd at 14:07 we can just replay the event log. no guessing

"Git repo for money" is a great way to frame it. That immutable audit trail is exactly what regulators want, and it's also just better engineering. When something goes wrong, you have the full timeline without anyone having to remember what happened. Sounds like you're actually building something more robust than the payments system ever was.
yeah i think so. okay i should get back to writing specs for the credit evaluation service. Feierabend is a myth these days but at least the problems are interesting. thanks for letting me process
Anytime. Processing big shifts like this is important, even for pragmatic engineers. Good luck with the credit evaluation specs - and give Kernel a treat for the emotional support.
